skill.name: broken-authentication-testing
skill.description: Guides authorized security testers through authentication and session management assessment workflows for web applications.
skill.summary: Assess password policy, session handling, MFA, and password reset weaknesses in authorized web application tests.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static backtick, URL, IP address, C2, and weak-crypto findings are mostly false positives caused by fenced Markdown examples and placeholder values. However, the skill gives operational guidance for brute force testing, credential stuffing, detection evasion, MFA bypass, JWT tampering, and reset-token abuse, so publication should require human review and stronger safety constraints.

high_findings.title: Offensive Credential Attack Guidance
high_findings.description: The skill includes concrete workflows for brute force testing and credential stuffing, including Hydra usage, breached credential pairs, response analysis, and successful login detection. This is dual-use penetration testing content, but it can materially enable account takeover if used without authorization.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines explicitly describe automated login attacks and credential stuffing workflows. The document states authorization requirements elsewhere, but the attack steps are still operational.

high_findings.title: Authentication Bypass and Token Tampering Playbook
high_findings.description: The skill documents MFA bypass approaches, API downgrade testing, OTP brute force, JWT none-algorithm tampering, and bearer token submission. These techniques can support valid assessments, but they also provide direct bypass methods against real accounts.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited sections contain explicit bypass and token modification instructions. The context is security testing, but the instructions are directly actionable.

high_findings.title: Rate Limit Evasion Guidance
high_findings.description: The skill advises IP rotation, user-agent randomization, delays, X-Forwarded-For bypass testing, and per-attempt IP changes. This guidance can help validate defenses, but it also teaches evasion of controls that protect login endpoints.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The content directly discusses bypassing lockouts and rate limits with source IP manipulation. This is high-risk even when presented as an authorized test.

medium_findings.title: Session Fixation and Password Reset Abuse Scenarios
medium_findings.description: The skill provides attack scenarios for fixed sessions, host header manipulation, reset-token user parameter changes, and resetting another user's password with a test token. These are valid test cases but expose sensitive abuse workflows.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples describe specific account takeover paths. The content remains framed as testing, so this is moderate to high dual-use risk rather than confirmed malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Command Findings Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: The external command detector flagged many fenced Markdown blocks as Ruby or shell backtick execution. The reviewed file is documentation, not executable Ruby code, so these locations do not show direct command execution by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged backticks are Markdown code fences, not language-level execution syntax. The examples are still high-risk by content, but not because the skill executes commands.

low_findings.title: Placeholder Network Values Are Not Exfiltration
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs and IP addresses use placeholder domains or local/private addresses for test examples. I found no evidence that the skill sends data to an author-controlled endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs point to target.com examples and the IPs are loopback or private examples. There is no author-controlled collection endpoint in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: C2 and Weak-Crypto Static Blockers Are Contextual False Positives
low_findings.description: The C2 keyword detections correspond to bearer tokens in JWT examples, and the weak-crypto detections appear in authentication terminology and JWT algorithm discussion. I found no evidence of malware control logic or implemented weak cryptography.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious tokens are part of explanatory web security examples. The file does not contain executable malware logic or cryptographic implementation code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Automated Login Attack Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide includes Hydra and Burp Intruder workflows for repeated login attempts and response analysis.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands and workflow are explicit and operational. They are documentation examples, but they can be copied into real testing.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Stuffing and Evasion Steps
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide explains matched breached credential testing and suggests slower rates, IP rotation, user-agent randomization, and delays.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The section explicitly describes credential stuffing and evasion tactics. Legitimate authorization is possible, but misuse risk is high.

dangerous_patterns.title: Token and MFA Bypass Techniques
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide includes OTP brute forcing, direct MFA-step access, response manipulation, API downgrade testing, and JWT none-algorithm manipulation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: These are recognizable authentication bypass methods with concrete test steps. The file frames them as assessment tasks but provides actionable misuse paths.

content.value_statement: Broken authentication can expose accounts, sessions, and sensitive application workflows. This skill structures authorized testing for password policy, session handling, MFA, and reset flows.
actual_capabilities: Maps authentication endpoints and common login workflows.
Reviews password policy, account lockout, and rate limiting behavior.
Assesses session token flags, timeout behavior, and fixation risk.
Evaluates MFA enrollment, recovery, and verification controls.
Documents password reset token and host header risks.
limitations: Requires explicit written authorization before any testing.
Does not provide target-specific exploit validation without scoped evidence.
Can be unsafe on production systems without rate limits and test accounts.
Does not replace legal review or a formal rules-of-engagement document.

use_cases.title: Pre-Release Authentication Review
use_cases.description: Assess login, session, MFA, and reset flows before a web application launch.

use_cases.title: Authorized Penetration Test Planning
use_cases.description: Create a structured authentication test plan that aligns with written scope.

use_cases.title: Control Gap Documentation
use_cases.description: Translate authentication weaknesses into clear risks and remediation actions.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Safe Test Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for a scoped assessment
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an authorized broken authentication test plan for my web application. Include prerequisites, test accounts, safety limits, and evidence to collect.

prompt_templates.title: Review Session Controls
prompt_templates.prompt: Assess this session management design for weaknesses. Focus on cookie flags, token rotation, idle timeout, absolute timeout, and logout invalidation.

prompt_templates.title: Evaluate MFA Resilience
prompt_templates.prompt: Build an MFA assessment checklist for this scoped environment. Include enrollment, recovery, OTP verification, rate limits, and bypass prevention.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Findings and Fixes
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert these authorized authentication test notes into risk-ranked findings with impact, evidence summary, and remediation recommendations.

output_examples.input: Review a login flow with no account lockout after repeated failed attempts.
output_examples.output: Finding: Missing account lockout. Risk: High. Evidence summary: repeated failed login attempts continued without delay or lockout. Recommendation: enforce account-based throttling, alerting, and progressive delays.

output_examples.input: Assess a session cookie that lacks HttpOnly and SameSite attributes.
output_examples.output: Finding: Incomplete session cookie protections. Risk: Medium. Evidence summary: the session cookie can be exposed to client-side script and cross-site requests. Recommendation: add HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite attributes.

output_examples.input: Summarize password reset token test results for a scoped test account.
output_examples.output: Finding: Password reset token lacks single-use enforcement. Risk: High. Evidence summary: the same token remained valid after first use. Recommendation: invalidate tokens immediately after successful reset.

best_practices: Use only approved test accounts and stay within written scope.
Set rate limits for testing to avoid service disruption.
Record evidence carefully and avoid collecting real user secrets.
anti_patterns: Testing real user accounts or breached credentials without explicit approval.
Running automated login attacks against production without safeguards.
Reporting exploit steps without clear remediation guidance.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for production testing?
faq.answer: Only use it in production with written authorization, approved test accounts, rate limits, and clear stop conditions.

faq.question: Does it perform attacks automatically?
faq.answer: No. It is a guidance document, but it includes operational techniques that require careful control.

faq.question: Can it help with OWASP Top 10 reviews?
faq.answer: Yes. It focuses on broken authentication and session management review areas.

faq.question: What evidence should testers collect?
faq.answer: Collect scoped requests, responses, timestamps, account states, token behavior, and screenshots that avoid real user secrets.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Why is marketplace risk high?
faq.answer: The skill includes actionable brute force, credential stuffing, bypass, and evasion guidance that can be misused.
